explanation: Laminaria digitata (kelp) extract is a brown seaweed botanical rich in fucoidan, alginate polysaccharides, iodine, and antioxidant polyphenols with hydrating, antioxidant, and skin-soothing properties. It is well-tolerated and used in marine-ingredient and protective formulas for its broad-spectrum antioxidant and moisture-supporting content.

Laminaria japonica (Japanese kelp/kombu) extract is a brown seaweed botanical rich in fucoidan, alginate, iodine, and antioxidant polyphenols, widely used in East Asian skincare for its hydrating, antioxidant, and barrier-supporting properties. It is well-tolerated and used in marine-ingredient formulas for its moisture-binding and protective content.

Laminaria saccharina (sugar kelp) extract is a brown seaweed botanical providing fucoidan, alginate polysaccharides, and antioxidant phenolics with hydrating and free-radical-scavenging properties. It is well-tolerated and used in marine-ingredient and protective formulas for its moisture-supporting and antioxidant content.

Lanolin is a natural wax from wool-bearing animals, composed of fatty acids, alcohols, and esters that closely mimic human skin sebum, making it one of the most effective occlusive emollients for sealing in moisture and repairing a damaged skin barrier. It is generally well-tolerated, though individuals with wool or lanolin sensitivity should approach with caution; ultra-purified medical-grade lanolin carries a significantly lower sensitization risk than crude lanolin.

Laureth-4 is a short-chain ethoxylated lauryl alcohol used as a surfactant and emulsifier in cleansers and rinse-off formulas. It is generally well-tolerated at typical concentrations, though its relatively low ethoxylation number makes it a stronger, more stripping surfactant than longer-chain laureth variants and may be mildly irritating on reactive or compromised skin.

Laureth-7 is an ethoxylated lauryl alcohol used as a mild surfactant and emulsifier in skincare formulas, providing cleansing and emulsifying activity with better skin compatibility than shorter-chain laureth variants. It is generally well-tolerated at typical cosmetic use concentrations.

Lauric acid is a 12-carbon saturated fatty acid abundant in coconut oil and palm kernel oil, used in skincare as an emollient with documented antimicrobial activity against Cutibacterium acnes. It has moderate comedogenic potential, making it a complex ingredient for acne-prone skin — valuable for its antimicrobial properties but potentially pore-clogging at high concentrations.

Lauroyl lysine is the lauric acid ester of the amino acid lysine, used in cosmetics as a dry, silky-feeling powder that improves the spreadability and skin adhesion of makeup formulas while providing a velvety texture to powders and foundations. It is well-tolerated, non-sensitizing, and considered a skin-compatible texturizer.

Lauryl alcohol (1-dodecanol) is a 12-carbon fatty alcohol used as a mild emollient and co-emulsifier in skincare formulas, contributing a soft, smooth skin feel and helping to stabilize emulsions. It is well-tolerated and non-sensitizing at typical cosmetic use concentrations.

Lauryl glucoside is an exceptionally mild, plant-derived alkyl polyglucoside surfactant from coconut fatty alcohol and glucose, used in gentle cleansers as a primary or co-surfactant with significantly lower irritation potential than sulfate surfactants. It is biodegradable, well-tolerated by sensitive and baby skin, and widely used in natural-positioned cleansing formulas.

Lauryl hydroxysultaine is a mild amphoteric surfactant used in gentle cleansers to improve foam quality, reduce the irritation potential of primary surfactants, and provide conditioning benefits. It is well-tolerated and commonly found in gentle, baby-safe, and sensitive-skin cleansing formulas.

Lauryl PEG-10 trissilylethyl dimethicone is a complex PEG-modified dimethicone copolymer used as a silicone emulsifier and texture modifier in water-in-silicone formulas, contributing a smooth, lightweight skin feel. It is well-tolerated and non-sensitizing, though individuals with PEG sensitivity should note the polyethylene glycol modification.

Lauryl PEG-8 dimethicone is a PEG-modified silicone copolymer used as an emulsifier in water-in-silicone formulas, enabling stable combination of silicone and water phases while contributing a smooth, lightweight texture. It is generally well-tolerated, though individuals with PEG sensitivity should note the polyethylene glycol modification.

Lauryl PEG-9 polydimethylsiloxyethyl dimethicone is a complex PEG-silicone copolymer emulsifier used in water-in-silicone and hybrid emulsion systems, providing a smooth, lightweight texture and excellent spreadability. It is well-tolerated and non-sensitizing, used in sunscreens and lightweight skincare formulas.

Lauryl polyglyceryl-3 polydimethylsiloxyethyl dimethicone is a PEG-free polyglyceryl-modified silicone copolymer emulsifier used in water-in-silicone emulsions, valued in clean-formula systems for providing silicone-phase emulsification without polyethylene glycol. It is well-tolerated and non-sensitizing.

Lavandula angustifolia (lavender) flower extract provides polyphenol antioxidants and soothing botanical compounds with anti-inflammatory and free-radical-scavenging activity. The extract form carries significantly lower sensitization risk than lavender essential oil — much of the volatile linalool and linalyl acetate is removed during extraction — making it generally well-tolerated for most skin types.

Lavandula angustifolia (lavender) essential oil contains high concentrations of linalool and linalyl acetate — both EU-regulated fragrance allergens — that can trigger allergic contact dermatitis and an itch-and-scratch cycle on reactive or fragrance-sensitive skin. Despite its widespread use and perceived naturalness, lavender essential oil is one of the more common causes of contact sensitization in leave-on skincare products.

Lavandula hybrida (lavandin) oil is a hybrid lavender essential oil with a higher camphor content than true lavender, containing linalool and linalyl acetate fragrance allergens with documented contact sensitization risk. It can trigger allergic contact dermatitis and an itch-and-scratch cycle on reactive or fragrance-sensitive skin, particularly in leave-on products.

Lavendula angustifolia oil — the alternate spelling and trailing period appear to be data artifacts from ingredient label parsing — is lavender essential oil containing linalool and linalyl acetate, EU-regulated fragrance allergens with documented contact sensitization risk. Despite the 'safe' classification in this entry, lavender essential oil can trigger allergic contact dermatitis on fragrance-sensitive or reactive skin, particularly in leave-on products.

Lecithin is a naturally occurring phospholipid complex derived from soy or sunflower that functions as an emulsifier, delivery enhancer, and skin-conditioning agent, integrating into the skin's own lipid bilayer to support barrier integrity. It is exceptionally well-tolerated, non-sensitizing, and widely used across all skin types in moisturizers and serums for its biocompatible, skin-mimicking chemistry.

Lens esculenta (lentil) fruit extract provides isoflavones, polyphenol antioxidants, and amino acids with free-radical-scavenging and skin-conditioning properties. It is well-tolerated and used in protective and nourishing formulas for its antioxidant content.

Lentinus edodes (shiitake mushroom) extract is rich in beta-glucans, kojic acid, eritadenine, and polysaccharides with antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and brightening properties. It is well-tolerated and used in anti-aging and brightening formulas for its combined beta-glucan soothing and kojic acid tyrosinase-inhibiting content.

Leptospermum scoparium (mānuka) leaf extract is derived from the mānuka tree native to New Zealand and provides leptosperin, polyphenols, and antimicrobial compounds with antioxidant and antibacterial properties. It is well-tolerated and used in protective and blemish-targeting formulas for its antioxidant and antimicrobial content.

Leptospermum scoparium (mānuka) leaf extract — the asterisk indicates organic certification — provides leptosperin, polyphenols, and antimicrobial compounds from New Zealand mānuka with antioxidant and antibacterial properties. It is well-tolerated and used in protective and blemish-targeting formulas.

Leptospermum scoparium (mānuka) leaf extract — the asterisk indicates organic certification — provides leptosperin, polyphenols, and antimicrobial compounds from New Zealand mānuka with antioxidant and antibacterial properties. It is well-tolerated and used in protective and blemish-targeting formulas.

Leucine is an essential branched-chain amino acid and component of the skin's natural moisturizing factor, used in skincare as a humectant and skin-conditioning agent that supports moisture retention and skin texture. It is biocompatible, non-irritating, and well-tolerated across all skin types.

Leuconostoc/radish root ferment filtrate is a natural preservation and microbiome-supporting ingredient produced by fermenting radish root with Leuconostoc bacteria, generating organic acids and antimicrobial peptides that inhibit pathogen growth while supporting beneficial skin flora. It is well-tolerated, non-irritating, and widely used in clean and natural-positioned formulas as a gentle fermentation-derived preservation alternative.

Lilium candidum (Madonna lily) bulb extract provides flavonoid antioxidants, mucilaginous polysaccharides, and skin-conditioning compounds with free-radical-scavenging and hydrating properties. It is well-tolerated and used in soothing and protective formulas for its antioxidant and moisture-supporting content.

Limnanthes alba (meadowfoam) seed oil is composed almost entirely of very long-chain C20–C22 fatty acid esters, providing exceptional oxidative stability and a dry, velvety skin feel. It is non-comedogenic, exceptionally well-tolerated across all skin types, and valued in skin and hair care formulas for its long-lasting emollient properties and superior shelf stability compared to most plant oils.

Limonene is a terpene fragrance compound found in citrus peel oils and many botanical extracts, classified as one of 26 known fragrance allergens required to be disclosed by name on EU cosmetic labels. While commonly perceived as natural, it is a well-documented contact sensitizer — particularly after oxidation — and can trigger allergic contact dermatitis and an itch-and-scratch cycle on reactive or fragrance-sensitive skin with repeated leave-on exposure.
